title: Type
text: Type Implementation type defined or available for the component. A component cannot have both Uniface and operating system implementations at the same time. However, a component with a Uniface or operating system implementation can also have one or more of the non-Uniface implementations. A component can have the following implementations: Table 1. Component Implementations Implementation type Operations available by default Editable Comments Uniface Component ACCEPT , EXEC , QUIT Y These are built-in Uniface operations. You can define parameters for the EXEC operation only. INIT , CLEANUP These are explicit operations. If you define operations named INIT and CLEANUP they are activated implicitly. For more information on INIT and CLEANUP see operation . You cannot define parameters for INIT and CLEANUP . C None N You can define the default operations ACCEPT , QUIT , and EXEC and the implicit operations INIT and CLEANUP . You cannot define parameters for ACCEPT , QUIT , INIT or CLEANUP . However, if you define both 4GL and C implementations for a component, the ACCEPT , QUIT , and EXEC parameters become available. If you then delete the 4GL implementation, these parameters remain available for the C implementation. COM None N No default operations are available. Java Call-in None N No default operations are available. Stored Procedure None N No default operations are available. Operating System Service COMMAND , COMMANDOUT N Enter operating system commands using ProcScript to be executed via an Operating System Service. You can use your assignment file to redirect the Operating System Service components to a Uniface server. The maximum length of a command is 511 bytes. Applies To Signature Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
